title: Educational fun if there be such a thing
text:
This is a fantastic Myst clone that, on top of everything, is "gladdened" with a load of physical minutiae which, naturally, lends the game its name. Now I find all matter of educational things to be a compelling incentive to wanting to solve the puzzle. This does not move me to finish the game, however. This, perhaps, is the greatest shortcoming for an adventure game. There is no plot as you know it. Yes, you need to achieve a goal and save the world from a global cataclysm. We've saved the world before, and it has been none too grateful getting into some sticky predicament time and time again. But WHO are you saving? Is it the disembodied voice in the introductory video? This is where the plot begins and ends. There are some marginal hints at a story involving a telescope lens thief, but these are clearly there to facilitate the puzzles. Yet, for a game that makes clear its ambition to create a giant puzzle, Physicus superbly succeeds to render the puzzles built on physical laws maximally enjoyable.
The physics information, like in all the games of this series, is presented in a kind of a very pretty virtual textbook which offers extensive interpretation of the physical concepts in optics, mechanics, electricity, fluid dynamics and several others. The textbook, if you choose not to strain your eyes, is read aloud by a throaty Englishman with a little interaction. There are interesting animations and illustrations on most pages; there are even some formulas which you would use in the game. Despite my enthusiasm, reading even one topic in a sitting is tiresome. On the other hand, if you follow the game's suggestions to read the topic in key scenes, you will miss on much of the textbook's info and that is annoying if you do like that sort of thing. In some cases the formulas come short in an attempt to make the game less mathematical. I had to intuit one puzzle because of the lack of one formula variable. Toward the end of the game there is perhaps too much simple-equation math regarding electricity generator transformers that needs to be muddled through to see the closing credits. Despite of how daunting it sounds here, all of this gruntwork was very enjoyable to me.
The graphics are faultless, but nothing inspired after seeing Myst. The music is equally insipid: good for a while, but nothing to write home about. I had to engage in a little bit of pixel-hunting in the beginning of the game. The game was on the short side unless you got stuck on a puzzle. It took me about 10 hours to finish it, and I did get stuck.
Overall, a great educational title; a good puzzle; an average adventure game. "A" for the effort.

title: No pocket Streets!
text:
You can't tell from anything in the description, but Microsoft very quietly decided to drop Pocket Streets from this edition. If you hadn't purchased any version of Streets and Trips before, you wouldn't be any the wiser, or if you didn't care about using it on a PDA, then this software might be OK, but since I bought it to update my PDA software (from Streets & Trips 2005), this package was pretty worthless to me. BTW, it turns out that Streets & Trips 2006 really only had Pocket Streets 2005, although they called it 2006. (This from Microsoft's tech support).

The one map segment I looked at wasn't very up-to-date: The street that our hotel was on hadn't made it into the database, and it's been there a while.

Also, when you try to install, it complains about an incompatible version of Windows Media Player. The folks at Microsoft also verified this, but had no solution other than to just continue on with the install.

At some point, Microsoft is going to come out with a separate Pocket Streets, which they will sell separately. Until then, you'll have to look elsewhere for a PDA solution.

title: Amazingly fun dungeon crawler!
text:
I have to say, I'm very happy with this game. It costs next to nothing, and it runs perfectly well on my Acer AO722 Netbook at moderate settings. The game reminds me of Diablo in so many ways! Some might think it's a knock off, but who cares?! It's fun as hell! Here's a list of some things that I like:

1) Gameplay is exactly what you'd expect from a dungeon crawler.
2) The town is a bit more active than what we experienced in Diablo 1 and 2; More quest-givers and shops.
3) The music is awesome.
4) I really like how the game isn't so complicated. It's a great way to unwind! You click on things, and they die!

Now for some minor annoyances:

1) Randomly generated (and expensive) equipment from shops coupled with the random drops you get might leave gaps in your equipment; some run-throughs will be very kind to you, and others won't. You could be stuck with a crappy item or 2 for several levels!
2) I only play this game on my netbook, so this might not be fair, but the load times are terrible! I'd say upward of a minute (maybe 2 minutes on occasion.)

I've played several dungeon crawls, ranging from The Legend of Zelda, the Diablo series, Dungeon Siege 3, Dungeon Hunter: Alliance, Baldur's Gate: Dark Alliance, and the Fallout one for PS2. This game competes very well with all of the titles I have just mentioned!

title: WORSE than the viruses it's supposed to protect against!
text:
Let me first start off by saying that you will consider any Norton product a virus if you try to uninstall it. It wraps itself up so thoroughly into Windows that removing all traces of it is nearly impossible without professional help.

Norton will write itself not only into multiple registry entries, but it will also add itself to the start routines of many kinds of software (i.e., Windows Explorer and Office Word) so that, even if you uninstall it via the recommended steps, you will still hang your computer as the browser and word processor sit and wait for a non-existent Norton to start. To REALLY get rid of this horrible bloatware, you have to completely edit the registries* or just lay down a clean install of Windows. Just do a search for "Norton uninstall" and you'll see endless forums of people agonizing over this...but Norton's support FAQ provides nothing more than some general tips (with no detailed troubleshooting) and a removal tool that doesn't remove the products completely. This is unacceptable!

Norton used to be the biggest name in computer security, but somewhere along the way they began thinking that making their product UNREMOVABLE without professional (read: their PREMIUM pay support!) help would be a good idea. The average user will be enraged by the system drain on resources and they will want to sue Norton when they can't completely uninstall the shoddy product.

It took me hours and a registry editor to finally banish most traces of Norton even after following the support FAQ's steps, but I still can't use Explorer without a window popping up repeatedly asking me to insert the Norton disc so it can reinstall the Norton programs to my computer.

NORTON PRODUCTS ARE LIKE VIRUSES THEMSELVES. If you can't uninstall the program without crippling your system and it won't remove itself at your request, then how is it different from a virus, especially when it will slow down processing while it's running? I will never buy another Norton product as long as I run Windows and I will encourage others to do the same. There are MANY other anti-virus programs like AVG and NOD32 that install and uninstall much cleaner than either this or McAfee.

A DEFINITE DO NOT BUY. YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED.

*If you edit the registry incorrectly, you WILL cripple your computer. You should really know what you're doing before you try this...but you can't really get rid of the program unless you do this, I've found. There are dozens of support forums where this process is explained, but you do registry editing at your own risk.

title: Move your PC into the Matrix
text: While taking graduate computer courses, at one point I had a whole network of servers and PCs running at home. I used Parallels to virtualize that network onto a single MacBook Pro. It worked well. (Be sure to have sufficient memory on your Mac before trying this.) Now I just have Windows 8 and Windows 7 in Parallels, but I've used Vista, XP, Ubuntu Linux, OS X, and several other virtual machines. I prefer Parallels to Boot Camp because it allows me to run Windows and Mac software simultaneously on my MacBook Pro. Also, I find that Windows runs much more smoothly on my MacBook Pro than it ever did on a dedicated PC.

title: Dishonest, incompetent tech support - Deductions app broken
text:
H&R Block At Home is a not worth your time or money, particularly if you need to account for charitable contributions. Even without contributions, you will not be able to get competent or even honest technical support.

I've been pleased with previous year's versions, but the 2010 version falsely claims it includes a working version of DeductionPro, a separate program for documenting deductions based on charitable contributions. For the 2010 tax year, DeductionPro is only available online. It does not work. I use a Mac and could not get DeductionPro to work with any browser. I tried Safari, Firefox, and Chrome. Yes - I turned off pop-up blocking for the DeductionPro sites. I was online with H&R Block At Home tech support for nearly 90 minutes. During the conversation, the support rep (who seemed to have zero IT expertise) repeatedly told me incorrect information, only to change his story when challenged (I am an IT professional, so I usually can detect bluffing from a support rep). First he said the product was not supported on Macs. When I read the statement from the side of the product box, he put me on hold and came back with, "Oh, it is supported on Macs, but only with Internet Explorer." That pattern of providing one answer, and then changing it when challenged, repeated throughout the call. Finally, I tried it using Windows 7 (since I have it also on my Mac) and the latest version of Internet Explorer, with the same failure. He then asked to put me on hold again, but hung up on me instead.

title: Want Lousy Software? This is it.
text:
I have had nothing but praise for PaintShopPro when the software was still owned by JASC. I finally upgraded to Corel PSP-X3 a short while ago to use for a project and I found it to be a buggy mix of ill-conceived, ill-designed features that somewhat mimic Photoshop without the performance and intuitive GUI.

PSP-X3 has an effects browser that does not undo the filters you browse when working, which could be catastrophic in a money-making task-- both from the perspective of lost time and possible destruction of original materials. I am glad that I never used the organizer feature, I don't know what might have happened to my files had I let that software fondle them.

The compression settings for jpg files would not stay where I put them, without compression, so, I had to set them for every "save" situation. Because of this, you should never use auto-save features on Corel PSP. The old JASC version never had these issues.

I uninstalled this junk. Sadly, I wasted my money on it, but, never again will I ever contemplate buying anything from Corel.

I wish there was an option for negative stars in this rating system.

For someone who knows what they want, and what they want to do, I would steer away from this. Programs like Handbrake, DVD Flick, and movie maker can do most of what you want, and for specific tasks the seasoned videophile will find something that meets their needs.

If you am not sure what you need, but want to cover your bases, this is an excellent start. For the experienced person, this may be a bit of an overkill, but is good nonetheless.

title: Great for limited uses and users
text:
Though the product has many advanced features and uses, the applications for which they can be used are very limited and the audience is very narrow. The cost is also a consideration because a lot of these features can be done as part of similar priced writing bundles.

When I first installed the program I was hoping for something to do mind mapping or idea mapping, what I found, however, was a program aimed at creative writers; writers of books, screenplays, and stories, with limited functionality for business or educational writing.

The learning curve is also very steep, requiring knowing numerous keyboard shortcuts for the features. Once you learn them, however, you find that this does a good job of not only organizing your ideas, but cataloging them and tracking their changes across time.

The included templates are adequate and are a good start for most creative writing applications.

The overall interface seems dates and looks like something from Windows 2000 or earlier, and doesn't fit with the elegance of new operating systems like Vista, 7, or even XP.

It also has limited import and export functionality. Copying and pasting from MS Word, for example is not allowed, unless the document is first saved into rich text format. Something I do not do in my business applications.

Lastly, the cost is a negative factor. For $99 you can have this product and load it onto three of your own computers, but you are not allowed to share copies. Perhaps for $99 they could allow you to load it onto 2 different computers with different users?

title: It does more than you could believe...
text:
The first thing that as a customer, I would need to know, is that the customer service at Ashley Software is amazing. They will write back and forth in emails at any time. I never had to get on the phone with them.
I was looking for a software that would allow me to write bits and pieces out of sequence, and then move these parts around until they were sequenced; i.e. dream journal. When I recieved the software, it was jaw dropping as to what it can do. You can make "note cards" that are note card size, or chapters long. You can print them. In the end these note cards can be turned into a document. You can make these blocks/note cards something that can be saved to a template, such as for a sales agreement, and then be able to write blocks around these blocks to incorporate newer informarion, or information that is specific to a particular item. It can write novels, research papers, screen plays. It has a smart dictionary that will learn names. You can make a list of most frequently used words, sich as Pharmaceutical...you don't want to type this ten times, so you have it recognized by the 4th letter, and it will be automatically selected. There is a "power bar", which is a double of a writer's blocks block. It has a setting that makes it "always on top". With this feature, you can browse the web for reliable facts. You can make a fact box by cut&pasting your selected data, and then copying the URL to later create a proper works cited, works consulted or bibliography page. I wish I had this tool 25 years ago!!
It is for a mind that needs to "get it all out" first, and organize later. I am thrilled with this software.

*A note on the item packaging. It is a key card only. The picture indicates this, but it can be confusing when you get the folder in the mail; there is a place for a CD. Disregard this space. The company makes all kinds of software and uses the same packaging. Also, if it seems used, as mine did...not shrink wrapped, and the round sticker was dirty and not wrapped over the two sides... this is most likely due to wearhouse storage at Amazon. I tweaked thinking it was used. Knowing that customer service is fantastic, try to register the product. If you have trouble at that point, contact Ashley Software and they will work things out. They do have a wepage where you can try the product first. 😎

title: The voice is great. The other software that gets installed, not so much.
text:
The "Amy" voice for text-to-speech has a very nice (maybe even soothing) female mid-range tone with a British accent. The inflection of the voice is more personable than a lot of TTS voices.

I'm very happy with the voice. I'm not so happy with the additional software that gets installed.

There seems to be a trend to take more and more of the decision making and choices away from end users. The Internet just gets more and more "pushy," and so does a lot of software. Some of it seems to claim your machine as its own personal property, putting on stuff you don't want, doing things you don't want.

I don't like proprietary downloaders and licensing software that phones the mothership and doesn't give you the choice to not install it. And this software does that. And it wants to put stuff on your context menus and browser.

Two readers get installed -- a "mini" freeware reader, and a full version reader with a thirty day trial and a nag popup reminding you how many free trial days you have left.

And the full version isn't all that. It's actually a bit clunky. It has a couple of advantages over a basic reader -- it will read SSML tags, and it will "read" a text file straight to MP3 format. (SSML tags are like HTML for text-to-speech, allowing you to customize the way certain parts of a document are spoken.)

The full version reader price is $39. Not too bad, but it seems overpriced because the markets are changing, now that you can get apps for mobile devices that do dozens of times more stuff, and run lots better, for just a couple of dollars.

And these are relatively small gripes, but the IVONA icons are just ugly, and "Amy's" digital art face that is automatically placed in mp3 tags as an album cover is rather "uncanny valley."

But the voice itself is great. I think it was overpriced, but I don't regret buying it. It sounds that good. I'm sure that in a few years it will sound crude and mechanical in comparison to what will be available in the future. But for now it's pretty nice.

title: Great concept, but way overpriced and inexcusably buggy
text:
I purchased Writer's Blocks 3 some years ago, thinking it the perfect solution for my writing style. I tend to write short notes then organize and assemble them into a sensible order for a finished manuscript.

Previously I had been using index cards and a manual system. Later I used programs like Azz Cardfile and other digital index card organizing systems.

Writer's Blocks looks good in concept, but for some reason execution never worked out well. I think it has to do with the very small size of the blocks and the limited amount of text displayed in each block -- it makes it difficult to get the bigger picture. Reducing the block text size may have seemed like a logical fix, but smaller text becomes too hard to read on the limited real estate of a computer display.

I remember another thing -- I paid a premium price for Writer's Blocks 3, and was surprised at how poorly supported the program was, especially for the price. Updates were few and very, very far between.

I downloaded the evaluation version of Writer's Blocks 4 to see if there were any major improvements. There are a few (like a window that displays all the contents of a block at once), but there is no way the program is deep or complex enough to be worth the price asked, especially since it is still a buggy program. For example, when converting a text file manuscript to blocks, block titles are often inserted randomly within paragraphs. And with the history of poor support of the program, I'd hate to pay full price for a program with bugs that may never be fixed.

I especially found it insulting that existing Writer's Blocks owners are being asked to pay nearly $100 for an upgrade to version 4. There's just not that much going on with the program to justify that. You'll find more complex and able programs for Android systems going for only a few dollars.

Writer's Blocks is a great concept, as I said, but I think I'll wait for someone else to write an app that accomplishes a similar thing and does it better, and with better support.

title: Norton Internet Security 2005
text: Installation is automatic. It changed several desktop icons to the square blue border window. It obliterated PanicWare, an excellent and free pop ad blocker. Zone Alarm disappeared. I had Norton systemworks 2005 installed and demanded reinstallation. I could not use email anymore with my Outlook Express, with no receiving and no sending e mails at all. To connect to the Web I had to disable Norton Internet Security first. Some icons from my desktop lost the shortcut properties and I could not start their associated programs. In summary: a total disaster. Solution: Remove the full program and reinstall Norton Systemworks. This program is as bad as it gets and will only tarnish the good reputation of Symantec.

title: Zero...is that a legal rating?
text: One star minus one...that gets you ZERO stars. Terrible. The Movie/tv options did not come close to meeting our viewing likes, it was more like a selection option of dislikes. And just so you know, this is not a commercial free zone. Not that this is bad, it’s just that I was not expecting it. Maybe I missed it in the agreement stipulations. It’s odd too, because these commercials do not even occur at what would seem to be a natural pause. Then try to cancel your subscription.... In January I contacted Amazon Customer Service asking for help canceling this subscription and was told I did not have a subscription to HULU, which I accepted, thinking that I may have checked a box that states my paid subscription does not automatically start until I actually check a box requesting it.  February comes and a monthly fee for HULU subscription appears once again as a debit on my bank account and is listed as HULU. So here I go again, searching the query “How to Cancel Hulu”  on the search field within the Amazon page itself. I was directed to a page where you have the option to purchase a book that tells you how to do just that, so I bought it. That’s right folks, I was desperate. It was an Unlimited Kindle book, so I was not not out any money, and am I ever glad because this book is a piece of junk, and did not give correct information. So here I am, back to the good old Amazon Customer Service, only this time I had good results. good results. Save your money on this one folks...the Book and the HULU subscription.  And at the end of the year, reward yourself with a trip to Best Buy to purchase some movies with your savings. Oh my oh my.......

title: Functional and affordable
text: I used Corel products years ago and I decided to go back to them after I retired. I no longer needed complicated office programs but I still needed something to create and manage my personal documents. WordPerfect Home & Office is just what I needed. I got the CD but preferred to download for the installation. The package installed easily and quickly. The word processor allowed the option of saving my current documents in the WP format or keeping the previous format. I must admit that I like playing with the graphics in Presentations. AfterShot is a good photo editing program for enhancing and manipulating. My only complaint is that when I was installing a program, it asked if I wanted to restart now or wait. I opted for wait but it restarted my computer, anyway. Other than that, all the programs work fine.

title: Takes a while to install
text: After a few tries, I finally got this installed on my laptop. It does take a while but it does work. The program itself does not take up too much hard drive space. The software calls for 4GB of RAM with 6GB recommended. My laptop has 8GB RAM, so it works well. The program opens quickly and locates stored video files fast. I recorded some videos with my Samsung S8+, so the quality is already good. I have not yet tried the editing features. I have only watched videos so far. The sound and the quality have been great. I set it as my default video viewer.

title: Comprehensive program for the serious amateur
text: This is a program loaded with features. I installed from the disc but there a download option if you have the key. My only complaint is that it takes a long time for the program to open. Once inside, there are tips and tutorials available. The 21-day access is not much use if I need it more than 21 days. There are YouTube videos to support the learning. I like these better because I can stop and replay indefinitely. The files loaded will be the quality of your camera. I tried files from my Samsung S8+ and my Canon EOS 80D. After the file is imported and stored in a bin, the imagination should take over. There is so much I can do to edit my files and when I am done, I can save them in any format I wish. The editing supports YouTube, Facebook and more. This is a great program for a beginner or the intermediate user.

title: A lot of learning for the value
text: I've been using Rosetta Stone for various languages. At first, it seems daunting and there is no rhyme or reason to the training, but it does work. I tried it for a few months, then left it alone. I went back to it a few weeks later and I was surprised at how much I remembered. I currently live in Africa and I travel to the Middle East. When I speak Modern Standard Arabic, everyone understands me. It's like speaking proper English-everyone who speaks it understands it. If you want to learn slang or street language, this product is not for you. This level is used by diplomats, business people and academics. News stations such as al Jezeera are easier to follow after learning this language. At first just listen to the vocabulary and don't try to understand the written language unless you are already studying the alphabet. Knowing the alphabet makes learning the vocabulary much easier. You should also get a grammar book because Rosetta Stone works based on the mind's intuitive learning ability. The photos are supposed to help you figure out what is being taught through association. Thankfully the price came down a little but you do get a lot of learning activities for the price. You get the program disk and the 3 levels of learning, a disk for loading additional modules on an MP3 player, and 3 months of online learning with games and other activities. This program does not work for everyone, just as any other program can't work for everyone. Of all the different programs I've tried, this is definitely the best. Find a way to try before you buy because it is a steep investment if it doesn't work out for you.

title: Fun With Photography — Cyberlink PhotoDirector 8 Ultra — Adds the Artistic Touch.
text: Cyberlink PhotoDirector 8 Ultra, I'm just learning how to use all the features. It looks a lot like Photoshop, some of the same features. I do not do video, but this has the video to photo capabilities. There are apps that go with this, for example: YouCam perfect — perfect selfies. There is YouCam Makeup which will map your face so you can add make up for a virtual makeover.PhotoDirector Mobile works with iPhone and the app is available on google play and can also be downloaded from the Windows Store. It has layer editing, pro filters, effects, and blurs. It will back up photos to CyberLink Cloud with 25GB of space free for a year. (Not certain how much it would cost after that?)  Camera RAW support, instant lens correction are some other features. Color Splash will turn images black and white while retaining selected parts of the photo in color. One-click toning auto tones and redistributes and balances your photos tonal values instantly. Body Shaping can remove unwanted sagging of bulges — getting rid of a belly that might be sticking out! Sublime Skin Tones gives the best natural look to a portrait. Powerful retouching tools can add sparkling white teeth, remove dark circles under eyes etc. There are Express Layer Templates make it easy to layer components to create stunning images. The Face Swap can take one face (perhaps with closed eyes) and swap it with another to create a single perfect photo. Mobile photo editing puts photo editing tools on your phone so you can quickly edit and add to Facebook. The regular desktop features are now on the go. A professional Photo Editing Course is also included, plus Photo Editing Tutorials. Now that should keep the most devoted photographer engaged in many activities. Even beginners in photography are encouraged to get started with the tutorials to guide you.

title: The best voice recognition s/w there is.  Perfect?  No.  But - quite amazing, overall.  12.5 is a substantial improvement.
text:
I tried DNS a few years ago and was pretty impressed, but I just didn't feel like making the effort - taking the time - to get it really working well at that time. A couple years later, I decided to see what kind of improvements have been made, so -

I bought the product and spent a little time - and I mean a _little_ time really - creating a user profile based on normal speech patterns and volumes, as well as getting a decent microphone/headset for under $50 (the microphone can make a _huge_ difference in the accuracy of ANY voice recognition setup).

I am AMAZED at how well it works. It actually works BEST, for me, when I speak at a very natural pace and cadence - if I try to slow down my speech or put effort in to enunciating a word it actually works worse. I speak rather quickly in fact (with today's hardware, the program has NO problem "keeping up", and actually works better the faster that I speak, at least to a point. Also - the ability to add to and alter the program's vocabulary - with my own voice speaking/pronouncing whatever word that was having problems - is awesome. Any errors or problem with recognition is corrected in a few moments - and it NEVER occurs again. After a few days of using it, and customizing the vocabulary (extremely easy and intuitive to do), the accuracy has climbed considerably. For normal dictation it is nearly flawless - stellar even. What's even sweeter is I have been able to make use of it to write software (been a s/w dev for decades.. wow - yep, decades now). I can easily code a fairly complex routine in C++, C#, Java etc.. with voice now.. piece of cake. With macros and templates - the only reason for typing now is to just keep up my WPM/chops for those times when DNS might not be available. And, Andrew, I guarantee that I have NO affiliation with Nuance or etc.. I enjoy bashing a sub-standard product or item as much as the next guy - honest. In this case - I know from my own experience that nothing else comes close to DNS for voice recognition, and that the amount of time required, for "setting up" one's voice profile and dictation environment, is quite reasonable. Within a day or two, provided one has a decent microphone and a computer that is at least a dual core (i.e. Core2 Duo), anyone can get this working quite well. With the level of customer support that these folks offer - I cannot believe that anyone would think this product "sucks" - unless they were working for a competitor, or had absolutely ZERO patience for configuring or setting things up. A joke comes to mind - but it is quite politically incorrect. It involves a person with a severe speech impediment complaining about their voice-recognition device.... If you cannot speak clearly and enunciate reasonably well - i.e. if other people have difficulty understanding you in your every day life --- then DNS may have trouble understanding you as well, although it will learn and improve if you spend the time.

Along the way - it has become clear to me that Nuance customer support is rather excellent. For all of the negative reviews and reviewers (some are just clearly negative - people, frankly) -- there is no other product that even comes close to DNS. I have at times been extremely annoyed at DNS, especially with older versions in years past - but in the end, I have to admit that it's quite a stellar product. Whenever it has a problem with one word or another, I can fix it via the vocabulary tool - speaking the word a couple of times - and it never fails to correctly recognize after that. Also - I use a Zoom HN4 hand-held recorder (an excellent device with quite high-quality audio) for dictation - and after a couple hours of tweaks, I'm very satisfied with the accuracy of the dictation.

title: "Might be the best product revision I've ever seen" - The Grinch (I stole Xmas if u recall)
text:
Please don't get me wrong. I am not a Microsoft fan. I think that their anti-competitive strategies and behaviors are .. "anti-consumer". I professionally use Linux and OS/X often, and there are several very nice word-processing choices available there these days. That said, I've worked professionally on Windows for ... well, since it was a silly little 16-bit thing that was about the only game in town. It's only appeal was as "eye candy" for the easily-impressed. Anyway - _that_ said, Word 2007 was, for me - in all honesty - the absolute best improvement to WORD that has been released. It required a shift in working habits, so for an hour or two was "unfamiliar", but the change from menus and drop-downs, etc.. (old-style Win GUI) to "The Ribbon interface", with what is now almost a standard, TABS for menus and feature-sets. Most complaints and negative reviews that I have seen, and I've seen MANY (all over the net), seem to be coming from people who just installed it, while they are still totally unfamiliar and in "withdrawal" from their old-habits. If these same people came back a week or two later, after actually trying to familiarize themselves with the "Ribbon", etc.. - I'm quite sure these reviews would be hugely more favorable.

Again, for ME, after just an hour or two - it's "hands-down" easier, far more convenient in terms of access to functions and features. Visual preview prior to making changes in format, etc... just ROCKS! For anyone who is even slightly "visual" in their organization and memory-function, this is going to be very pleasurable. I actually enjoy using it - which is something I never could have said about a word processor before. I'm very visual, in the way I organize and the way I access my memory. This type of interface really lends itself to that. And - customizing the interface for my particular needs is VERY easy and almost powerful. I have taken my most-used features/commands, and built my custom command tool (called the "Quick Access Toolbar"). Truth is, almost everything is quick-access with this version. Personally, I have previewed the more recent versions (post-2007, e.g. 2010), and they don't stack up. Not for the money for sure. 2007 is the, well, .. the "money" release. It's the one to get if you are using older versions. "SAVE-TO PDF" is a killer feature if you want to make content for Acrobat/PDF-Reader folks (internet documents, let's be honest); being able to save in any previous format, for compatibility with other users you know (I sent a doc to my bro-in-law. He emailed me back "you sent me a .docx file, and my Word doesn't know what that is...". I just saved as a ".doc" file (no-brainer) and sent it again, problem solved. This was 4 years ago. Anyway - if you are a Word user, and you're using any prior version, then you should consider 2007. Cheap, comparatively, and way better. More fun as well. Of course, if you're holding on to that old Pentium I from the 90's? Well, you match the software to the hardware, and vice-verse. ;-)

title: Makes a difference for students with learning difficulties
text: I used this program for several years in a specialized school.  The first year, I didn't tell any of my team teachers about the software.  I approached it like an experiment.  Within a few weeks, teachers began to ask me what I was doing differently.  They saw a marked difference in my students' focus and retention.  I only used it a few minutes (10-15 minutes maximum) two or three times a week.  The students varied the type of activity daily as instructed.  It is not for entertainment and should be used in short segments.  I highly recommend this program for building retention, focus, and auditory skills.

title: I love this app
text:
So I love this app and use it all the time. But recently, I needed to send my (android) tablet in for repair. Silly me deleted Amazon App Store (app) from my tablet because I was trying to minimize personal information on my tablet.

That was when I found out that this app will *not* work without the Amazon App Store app. This might not normally be a problem, but I cannot put the Amazon App Store app back on my tablet. It can't be directly downloaded, the link that Amazon gives you doesn't actually go to the app, but to a page for you to put in your email address for them to send the link. The email that they send goes back to the same page, asking for an email.

In short, I can't get the Amazon App Store App, which means that this program won't work unless I buy it again elsewhere.

title: Can't Install to Even See a Feature
text:
First of all – this software takes FOREVER to install. I’m talk over an hour or more. This is not going to be a “pop in the DVD and it’s done”.

You will need an internet connection for some of the install. I found this the hard way when my computer ended up on AIRPLANE mode. And, even when it did connect, it was SLOOOOW.

Then, no less than five times did I set a “setup error” pop-up. I even ran the repair, then I had to just uninstall the software.

But, that was not easy with the software. I ended up deleting the files from my computer manually.

That STILL didn’t get the software deleted. So, I tried the uninstall tool – again, an error.

Popped disc out and popped it back in. Maybe it was a bad read.

After popping the disc in my drive began going what I’d think was 200mph.

Tried the REPAIR tool … again …

Got six (6) SETUP error windows. The tool finished and closed.

So, in the first 60+ minutes – nothing accomplished. And, this was on a BRAND NEW, OUT-OF-THE-BOX, 4 day old computer.

It could have been a BAD disc. After all, if it was my drive, it wouldn’t have read the disc to begin with.

I can’t attest to the features of the software since it couldn’t be installed.

I’ve always had issues with software from COREL, and thought after 18 years it would be better – not this go around.

title: Great teaching tool!
text: I love the Rosetta Stone better than any app for learning a language. It is very thorough and uses multiple ways of learning. The exercises use pictures, audio, written, and pronunciation tasks. It can be as slow as you want to take it or faster paced. You can focus on basics, general, or specific areas. It’s perfect for me because I need a lot of repetition to learn. And you can go back over exercises you’ve already completed if like me you may get busy for weeks at a time and forget to do this on a regular basis. I highly recommend this if you are serious about learning to speak or read another language.

title: Meets all learning styles
text: I took 3 years of French in high school and didn’t really do well due to the way it was taught. I am a mostly visual learner, audio alone doesn’t work. I’ve heard as most people how great Rosetta Stone is and I am a believer. I love how the lessons work with this. Pictures, listening, and reading! And then having me pronounce words and phrases and letting me know if my pronunciation is correct. The lessons keep incorporating previous lessons. I can see why this is so highly recommended. I look forward to more lessons!

title: Limited Functionality
text: Let me start by saying that generally I think Turbo Tax is great.  It has made doing my taxes so much easier.  Turbo Tax guides you through easy, sensible questions to find all the deductions you're entitled to, and makes sure you report what you need to report.  So, on Turbo Tax generally, I'd give 5 stars.  This Basic-level product, however, has limited functionality.  My taxes are not very complicated and I don't qualify for the basic level.  Chances are good that you're going to have to upgrade on the website anyway.  My advice is by all means use Turbo Tax, but do it through their website, so you don't make a purchase and then have to purchase something else anyway.  Also, note, this does not include state taxes.  You'll pay an extra $40 per state for that.

title: Great Software
text: I've used Bit Defender software in the past, and I was pleased to try out the 2011 internet security suite.  I've been using the 2010 version of this product, and the 2011 version offers many of the same benefits.  It was easy to install on my PC, and it seems to protect my computer well.  It's easy to schedule scans.  The 2011 version offers a few new features.  My favorite of them is the feature that shows the anticipated time remaining on an anti-virus scan.  I like knowing if I'm in for 20 minutes or 2 hours.  In sum, I've been very happy with Bit Defender products in the past, and the new internet security suite continues to perform.  I'll be staying with Bit Defender for my internet security.

title: Great Computer Security
text: I've been very, very happy with this product.  I already knew that BitDefender had a good reputation, as this is what the guys at the local computer store have been recommending for anti-virus software, so I was pleased to try the 2010 version for myself.  This software was very easy to install- just insert the CD and click through a few times.  It offers convenient features, like the scheduling the time for virus scans and updates.  What's possibly most impressive to me is that it provides good security protection without messing up any of the other applications I use online, as some anti-virus programs do.  Overall, I see myself sticking with BitDefender for anti-virus software in the future.

title: Best there is
text: Norton is known for being the best anti-virus software around. I work in IT, and I've dealt with viruses and network penetration issues. Granted, the human element is the first real line of defense, but on the software side, having the right tools acting as guardians of the gate is critical. I always argue for Norton. I always choose Norton. For my own hardware, I never go with anything but Norton. It's not worth the risk to go with anyone else.

title: Good for photo editing
text: I always underexpose, partly because I was taught to and partly because that's a flaw I had before I was told it was a feature. This is great software for bringing back the highlights and brightening the image without losing the purity or saturation of the colors. It's no Photoshop, but for casual photo editing, it's fine.

title: Top of the line
text: Norton is by far the best out there, and the five device license can usually suit most families. The password manager component of the package leaves a lot to be desired. I was unable to set it up and get it to store passwords, fill passwords, generate passwords, or do much of anything. The web screening is good if I'm iffy about going to a site, but it won't use an engine other than Bing and Bing can barely find itself. The anti-virus, that's its main feature and that's where it shines. I work in IT and I'll tell you, there is nothing better. I will never trust another anti-virus software with my most imporant equipment.

title: Beginner friendly
text: I would highly recommend taking an online class to go with this. Udemy has quite a few. Probably other places as well. There is a learning curve here. I started with this, Procreate on the iPad, and Photoshop at about the same time, and I found this one to be the most beginner friendly. I can't say if it's the most powerful. It may be that I will later hit some limit that this can't do and the others can. That is common with the beginner friendly option. I can say that I was able to come up to speed fastest on this one and actually do stuff with it. I use it with an off-brand drawing tablet, and it works very well. I'm having a great time with it.

title: A GM's best friend.
text:
Quick lookup for anything Pathfinder. You can keep multiple tabs open at once, labelled and easy to switch between. You can search for anything across all the books. Weapon damage and costs. Monster stats and attacks. Anything. If you suddenly need to know the cost of a climbing kit, even if you're sending your players off into miles and miles of level plains, you can get to it by searching 'climbing kit' and it pops right up. So much faster than the mediocre indexes in the print books.

It's a must have for the Pathfinder GM, and a very good to have for any player.

title: As good as the web site
text: It hasn't given me any trouble, and it controls the home easily and quickly.  I use it to shut down all the lights at night (so I don't trip over the very dark tortie cat that likes to dart under my feet once I've turned my bedroom light off).  It lets me manage the modes, automations, everything.  Stable and convenient.

title: Great casual game
text: This is a weirdly addicting, simple but fun game.  Add 1s and 2s to get threes, then add like numbers to get the highest numbers possible before you run out of space.  Like many great games, it is simple in concept and endlessly entertaining.

title: The first name in anti-virus
text:
Norton has long been considered the gold standard for anti-virus software, and with good reason. It does a great job of protecting your system and being invisible while it does so. Yeah, there are pop up notifications of monthly reports and the like. You can shut them off in the configuration options. Once you do that, it's pretty much set it and forget it. If it sees you're running the system hard (gaming for example) it will throttle back to minimum operations, just enough to keep your system safe, to cut down on how much impact it has. If your system is idle, it'll perform a variety of optimization tasks, such as cleaning up temporary files.

I've always depending on Norton and it hasn't let me down. I run it on large, 7 TB desktops and tiny netbooks. It scales without a problem (and I can't think of anything else I could run on a netbook that would do the job without clogging the system). Norton is my first choice, every time.

It's not the best option for backups -- you only get two gig. For all other automated computer maintenance tasks, it's all you need.

title: Conveniently steam video from your pc to your tv
text:
I'm enjoying this. I have had trouble in the past showing home movies on the TV. I either have to find a way to convert them to DVD, or plug the computer into the TV and go through all that configuration, or plug the camera into the TV and figure out why it's not working. With this, I just select the tv, select the video, and there it is. I have testing streaming to an LG TV, and the TV simply tells me the feed is coming in, and do I want to display it. I click yes, and there is the video. There is a caveat here. You have to leave a short delay before your video for the clicking of yes, else your guests will miss the first minute or so. It can upscale to 3D, but it looks like a video that has been upscaled by a computer to 3D -- amusing, but not such a great thing. It can play DVDs to the TV. It can play selected sources, such as YouTube to the TV.

The only thing I disliked, and here is where I ding it a single star, is that it cannot stream custom sources from the Internet to the TV. I subscribe to The Anime Network, and would love to have used it to stream that and maybe some video podcasts that are not on YouTube.

title: The future of comics
text:
I've been a fan of Comixology since before the Fire came out, reading comics on the iPod Touch. You can buy on one device and read on another. You can buy online and read on a device.

The good. No "out of print" or "out of stock" issues. No endless long boxes clogging up the closet. No hunting for a particular issue. Let the app organize your collection. You can carry your entire collection if you want and pick up comics anywhere, anytime. Long out of print comics (as in the first Action or Detective from decades ago) are available cheap. Customer service is always top rate. The guided view technology makes comics easy and pleasurable to read, even on a small phone screen.

The bad. There are some synching issues with the Fire, always easily resolved. The DC comics aren't yet available for purchase (they are to read) on the Fire, so you can buy them on the website and download them to the Fire as a temporary workaround. You can use the browser in the Fire for this (that's how I do it) so it isn't much of an inconvenience, and they have announced they are working on it.

This is bleeding edge technology, so expect all the joys and troubles associated with such.

title: Learn to speak Spanish, not translate
text:
One of the hardest things to overcome in language learning is the desire to translate. If someone says "la mujer bebe leche" you don't want to think "the woman drinks milk" you want to derive the meaning right from the Spanish, and to go from intended message to Spanish when you speak. Translating will throw off your speed and can mess up your grammar, if the grammar of your native tongue is different from that of the language being studied.

There is almost no English in this. Instructions, where needed, are in English, and the rest is in Spanish. You learn the word for "woman" and the word for "man", then you get a picture of a woman drinking and the program says "la mujer bebe" (the woman drinks) and you click on the woman drinking. It never explicitly teaches the word for "to drink", you just figure it out from context. The upside--you stay in Spanish while learning Spanish. The downside--the brain cheats, so you only need to understand part of the sentence to get the right answer. The program exposes the learner to the same word in a variety of ways to help instill it.

The program is engaging, entertaining, and effective. It's the next best thing to taking a Spanish class.

title: Installed easily. Works well
text:
I installed this on an HP laptop running Vista Ultimate 32 bit.

The good.

The installation and setup is fast and easy.

It isn't a resource hog. It's clearly designed with the netbook user in mind, thus the USB drive for installing the software. It's well-optimized for these underpowered machines, and won't adversely affect performance like certain anti-virus software can.

The bad.

The license is only for one machine, which makes it more expensive than most alternatives. I like [[ASIN:B0026PEP5S Vipre AntiVirus + AntiSpyware]] best for value and efficiency.

Overall, not a bad selection, particularly for netbooks.

title: They need better Q.A.
text:
The program is buggy. Sometimes it doesn't do what it's supposed to. Often it crashes, and crashes badly enough that it requires a system restart. I've installed it under Vista Ultimate and XP Home, and both gave me trouble. I ended up not able to do much with it between crashes, and after a few hours, I gave up. It's a great concept and a decent price, but they need to take it through the Q.A. process again and get rid of more of the bugs.

Wait on this one. If they patch it, then consider it.

title: if using Apple products, check your models first ...
text: Well ... my mistake.  I chose this, thinking it was for a MAC and would work for me.  You need Sidecar to be able to operate it.  Sidecar is only available on the 5K 2015 models and later.  I did not realize mine was so "ancient" lol.  I have a retina 4K 21.5" late 2015.  I am getting ready to upgrade my computer this year, but that does not help me now.  Installs fine direct from the website.  A little finicky, but ultimately was ok.  Check your model before purchasing.  Will update this when I get it working fully.

title: excellent!
text:
My husband got a new Windows laptop and of course, you have to buy into the yearly Word/Excel/Powerpoint thing if you want to use those programs. I always hated how Microsoft forces your hand on paying for those "needed" programs and so, me personally, I switched to a Mac where Numbers and Pages are free.

But my husband uses a couple of Windows based programs that he needs what he needs. Thus, he has a Windows based laptop. Not wanting to support Microsoft in their seeming monopoly, I chose this Corel program for him. I have used Corel Wordperfect since probably the 80s. Our family had a personal computer in the late 70s. We also used Corel at our law office for years and years. It seems to be the preferred word processing program for the legal field. I purchased this hoping, hoping, HOPING my husband would like it.

So far so good! We likes it because he can be sent a Powerpoint file and he can open, edit, and play it in Corel. Nice! On his previous laptop, we were using an ancient copy of Office 2007 that was still working on his computer. On his new laptop however I could not get it to load. Too old I guess. His concern was Powerpoint and that one was a win. Word & Excel files can be easily converted to all formats for other people to read.

Everything about this version is nice and updated. No regrets so far. Again, I am not a Microsoft fan, not a Windows fan, so Corel really floats my boat for word processing. Would easily recommend for others.

title: GRRRRRR
text: I'm with the others.  DON'T DO IT.  I work remotely and tie in to a law office in a different state.  The computer crashed so they bought a new one with WINDOWS 8.  AH!  You can't find anything. All I wanted to do was go to the start menu, find my drive, and burn a CD with files.  Normally an easy task.  Not with 8.  Where IS the drive?  There isn't a tile, there isn't a start menu.  Where it used to take a few steps to do something, it now takes 100.  I have used Microsoft OS's FOREVER, but when my laptop dies, I'm buying a MAC.  If I have to completely relearn to use my computer, I mind as well learn to use a MAC.  I've been using computers since 1977 when it was a Commodore Pet 2001 and it had a tape player, and Windows 8 to me, is the most ridiculous piece of garbage operating system ever.  Maybe if you are a new user or someone who surfs the net it would be great, but forget trying to conduct REAL business with it.

title: Complicated Registration
text:
While I like most Corel products, this one is unimpressive.
Just about everything it does can currently be done with an app, and the fact that registration is a headache only adds to the frustration.
Still, if you just want the learning experience so that you have it under your belt, you might find this fun.

title: Solid Typing Program
text:
While this isn't the Mavis Beacon I've known and loved since her humble beginnings in 1987, it's still a decent program to learn or simply use to brush up on your qwerty typing.

Gone are the games and 'fluff', this Mavis (who, btw, is a fictional character named after the developer's favorite singer, Mavis Staples)is all business.
This is a good, no nonsense way to learn.

title: Good for simple cards or brochures

This early edition of PagePlus certainly came with its share of bugs and quirks.
Thankfully, more recent versions are more streamlined and intuitive.

One of the biggest problems with this one was a clumsy installation process and registration.
PagePlus can be fun, just get a newer version than this.

title: For those that learn Visually
text:
Sometimes pouring over page after page is difficult.
If that describes you, this is a great tool for learning or brushing up on Lightroom. Converting files, how to back up, it's a LOT of information. Not always as clear as I would have liked, sometimes you just want something streamlined. Still, nice to have this format as an option.
Tim Grey has written quite a few books on Photoshop as well as this video tutorial, which now has a newer version.

title: Pinnacle Studio 17 Plus has a large number of effects, transitions and other material to make movies
text:
[[VIDEOID:2875c8fdb5414aeadeb7146f1a953281]] Pinnacle Studio 17 Plus has a large number of effects, transitions and other material to make movies. It doesn't have all of the bells and whistles that some other movie editing software has, but it has a well-rounded selection for a reasonable price. If you are a novice in home video editing but want to step up from your simple minimalistic editing program that came with your camera, for example, this is a good choice. It introduces you to the interface of a more professional editor without the depth to get you lost quickly.

The setup is very user friendly. Mine asked for a DirectX Update immediately. You can decide if you want it to install all the Bonus Content. You will get prompted to update the software the first time it opens.
When you finally open the software after the update the application opens up with "The-Sky-is-the-Limit.Movie." The movie has music, images, video, and effects. You can use this video to learn how everything is organized. I suggest you save it to a new project name if you want to experiment with it.
In the timeline just move the slider to go to each part of the movie where there is an effect and you can then see it on the timeline. You can also scroll to the end to see how the end title is handled. In the title timeline (lower part of the window) you can double-click on the effect and get more detail about how it was done.

See the attached video to get more details. The video was done using the Live Screen Capturing application that came with Pinnacle Studio. I then took the video that was created and brought it into Pinnacle Studio and converted it to MP4 format for Amazon. All very straightforward. I will be using this application in the future as it offers me more options.

title: Might slow you down, but so will an attack
text:
With the volume of harmful threats that lurk around every corner of the Web, and malware that can find its way onto your computer you must have a product like this.
The initial scan takes a long time so be patient. Once that is done the software runs quietly in the background.
Since there are prominent competitors of this type of software I reviewed respected industry comparisons (security benchmark/review articles will lead you to them), and this product was among the best.
This new 2014 edition is very resource hungry and can slow your system down considerably so you need to review all the settings to optimize use or you will be shutting it down periodically to get work done. There is a price to pay for complete protection. And of course, be sure and completely remove any prior antivirus or internet security products before installing Kaspersky.

title: Perfect WordPerfect
text:
If you're used to using WordPerfect, and don't want to change to the more common similar program, you'll love upgrading with this very reasonably priced product.
If you are new, this is user friendly and easy to install.

title: Uninstall? I think not.

Like most Norton products, installation is pretty easy
It's the UNinstall that can be a nightmare. Even if you get it because of the current low price, make sure you really want it.

While the dash is decent, and the auto uninstall is good, there wasn't much difference despite the 'clean-up'.
While I did not expect a miracle, it was just ok.

title: Bang for the buck

For the price, I doubt you can do better than this.
From your smartphone to laptop to tablet, Norton 360 offers nice protection on an assortment of devices. I did NOT install on a laptop running Windows 8, as it looks like those bugs might not have been worked out, but the same can be said for Windows 8 itself. Very good value.

title: I like Dragon, HATE the hoops I had to jump through
text: I had an earlier version of Dragon on my PC, that never did install properly. I was THRILLED with Dragon 12 for PC which installed easily and works beautifully. In comparison, on my macBook Pro it is no better than the voice recognition preinstalled on my iPhone. Also, fighting to get it installed was a nightmare, but not any fault of Dragon. I had to remove and install Lion, took forever and what a pain. It isn't recognizing me as well as the PC version, which I find odd.

title: I'm thrilled to say...
text:
... That I'm writing this review without touching a key. I had the previous version of Dragon software (11), but I can't tell you much about it because I never was able to install it correctly. I kept getting a message that the disc was corrupted. The company even sent a new DS and I received the same message. The computer that it was being installed on was an HP touch smart. I'm thinking that perhaps the touch smart had something to do with the Dragon software problems. I got it on sale, and just chalked it up as a loss.

When I received this Dragon software the premium 12, I didn't let it come near my HP. Instead, I easily installed it on my Asus computer. After a quick tutorial, which was actually about 20 minutes ago, here I am, and I want to shout "look Ma, no hands!"

Looking back at the above I see that the word disc was understood by the Dragon software as DS. I will be updating this review, and perhaps adding that extra star, or taking stars away, as I learn all of the ins and outs of this program. I just wanted to get the review out there now so that you can see that it was easy to install and use right away.

PS... I'm amazed that Dragon knew that Asus was, well, Asus!
UPDATE:I've been using my Dragon software quite a bit and the more I use it the more I absolutely love it. Once again I'm using it to type the update to this review. One of the things I really love is called a "dictation box". It's a perfect little cheat sheet that's tucked away on the corner of my screen, always available to remind me of the various commands that I can give Dragon. Also, the included headphones that is very comfortable, and you're able to swing the microphone portion exactly where you need it. I had started out giving this software for stars, or shall I say 4 stars. In order to do that, I had to say "numeral 4", LOL!
I love it, so 5 stars it is! I have a lot to learn, but learning it is really fun.
If you or someone you know has failing eyesight, consider getting and learning this software NOW, to make it easier in the future. Good Luck, everyone!

Another update 11/29/12: Still doing well, the program is 'learning' how I pronounce certain words, and when I mean 'Pat', as in a person, as opposed to 'pat'.

title: Great on one of the PCs....
text:
....but on my little HP Vivienne Tam Mini, boy does it slow her down.
I installed this on an HP Touchsmart and a Mini. I used only the online instructions, because it is recommended to install in that manner, and the Mini doesn't take a disc anyway. Installation was fast and easy on both machines, and starts with a sweep of each machine. No problems with the Touch, but the Mini can barely load a page if a scan is in progress, and it's slower in general.
If you like great big alerts when something is caught, you will not like the subtlety of this Mcafee product, but personally I like it.
UPDATE: My little mini's hard-drive blew, so perhaps it was that which was slowing her down. Also, on the HP Touch, I love that it pops up when portions of a site are blocked.

title: AutoCAD LT2012, much improved!
text:
Lots of improvements with this version. There are new array features, which I liked a lot...especially being able to copy along a path with different spacing options.
The help commands are also much better. Even if you don't know AutoCAD's terminology you can pretty quickly find out how to do what you need. This is very helpful if you're coming from a different software.
Some of the commands are still buried, like creating a new layer. I know you can use quick keys but it could also easily be accomplished with the right click menu.
So, it doesn't matter if you're familiar or new to AutoCAD, with a bit of work you'll master this.

title: It CAN be used with an iPad!
text:
I started out skeptical. I was ready to point out every flaw...but after a while I LOVE it. As long as you have less of the expectation of the infomercials, this is an EXCELLENT introduction to a new language.

There are so many small yet super innovative things about this program. For example, if you try over and over to get the pronunciation right and keep failing, not only can you click an icon to hear it again, but you also have the option to open a screen that will playback your voice and show the 'voice printout' alongside the correct printout! How cool is that?! After finishing a lesson that includes some basic words I was brave enough to try the interactive games. And the games are so fun! I spent last night online for hours playing games with other Rosetta Stone users in other countries. They match you with similar levels of people so you never feel 'stupid'.
I can not roll my 'r' no matter how hard I try. It can get discouraging when over and over you get the noise indicating a mistake. I switched from the included headphones to the mic on the computer and then my less-than-perfect 'rr' was accepted.
If the program freezes, no worries, just shut down and re-open. That only happened once for me. Other than that, it was easy to load.
There is also a feature (FREE!!!!) called Mobile Companion. You download a speaking lesson for 'on the go', when you aren't on the computer.
I won't go into any more of what it does, because if you are reading you probably already know.
Now, if you have an iPad, not an iPhone or iPod, but the iPad, you will see that there is a note on the Rosetta Stone box indicating you can use the Mobile Companion with an iPhone or iPod. I followed the online instructions to get it to load on my iPad, without success. I accessed the tech support, which was easy and they were very polite, but was told there was no way to load on the iPad. I found a way!!! Do not follow the website directions. On your iPad, do NOT go to iTunes as they tell you to do, go to Apps. Then search NOT 'totale', but 'Rosetta Stone', and choose the free app. It will ask for your email and password, and then you're in! Just remember, it is only for 3 months, then an additional fee applies. A good tip...when you first install this program on your main computer, do not select the option to start your 3 month online support. Wait until you feel confident enough to take advantage of the games, live online lessons with other students (!!!), etc. Then log in to the 3 month extras. You will want every day of them.
Is it worth the money? Rosetta Stone pretty much has the market on home language learning. It can only be installed on ONE computer, and you can have up to 5 users. Do not buy thinking you can share it with your friends, or even with a family member on an unshared computer. If the whole family uses it on a shared computer, it is certainly worth it. If you like all the extra little touches that no one else has on a language program, it's worth it for the individual.

I have to go now. In a few minutes I have a live online lesson that I scheduled!
